title: End of module: Theory of Computation

questions.text: An FSM accepts binary strings ending in "01". States: $S_0$ (start), $S_1$, $S_2$ (accepting). Transitions: $S_0\xrightarrow{0}S_1$, $S_0\xrightarrow{1}S_0$, $S_1\xrightarrow{0}S_1$, $S_1\xrightarrow{1}S_2$, $S_2\xrightarrow{0}S_1$, $S_2\xrightarrow{1}S_0$. Tracing from $S_0$, does it accept the string "1011"?
choices: Accept
Reject
questions.explanation: Tracing "1011" from $S_0$: read 1 → $S_0$; read 0 → $S_1$; read 1 → $S_2$; read 1 → $S_0$. The path is $S_0 \to S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_0$, ending on $S_0$, which is not accepting — the FSM **rejects** "1011" (it ends in "11", not "01").

questions.text: Using the same "ends in 01" FSM as above, trace the string "10101" from $S_0$. Counting the start state, how many states does the path pass through in total?
questions.explanation: Tracing "10101" from $S_0$: read 1 → $S_0$; read 0 → $S_1$; read 1 → $S_2$; read 0 → $S_1$; read 1 → $S_2$. The path is $S_0 \to S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_1 \to S_2$, which is 6 states in total (the start state plus one per input symbol).

questions.text: An FSM reads a binary string most-significant-bit first and accepts iff the number it represents is divisible by 3. It has states $R_0$ (start, accepting), $R_1$, $R_2$ tracking the remainder mod 3: reading bit $b$ in state $R_i$ moves to $R_{(2i+b) \bmod 3}$. Does it accept "1010" (the number 10)?
choices: Accept
Reject
questions.explanation: Tracing "1010" from $R_0$: read 1 → $R_1$; read 0 → $R_2$; read 1 → $R_2$; read 0 → $R_1$. The path is $R_0 \to R_1 \to R_2 \to R_2 \to R_1$, ending on $R_1$, not $R_0$ — reject. This matches $10 \div 3$ leaving remainder 1, not 0.

questions.text: Using the same divisible-by-3 FSM as above ($R_0$ start/accepting, moving to $R_{(2i+b)\bmod 3}$ on reading bit $b$ in state $R_i$), does it accept "1111" (the number 15)?
choices: Accept
Reject
questions.explanation: Tracing "1111" from $R_0$: read 1 → $R_1$; read 1 → $R_0$; read 1 → $R_1$; read 1 → $R_0$. The path is $R_0 \to R_1 \to R_0 \to R_1 \to R_0$, ending back on $R_0$ — accept. This matches $15 \div 3 = 5$ remainder 0.

questions.text: Which of the following languages can be recognised by a finite state machine (i.e. is a regular language)?
choices: Balanced parentheses nested to arbitrary depth
Binary strings containing an equal number of 0s and 1s
Strings that start with the prefix "10"
$a^n b^n$ for an arbitrary number $n$
questions.explanation: Checking a fixed prefix only ever needs to remember "how many of the first two symbols have I matched so far" — a small, fixed amount of information, so a handful of states suffice. The other three all require counting an unbounded quantity (nesting depth, or a running difference/count that grows with the input), which no finite number of states can track.

questions.text: Select every language below that requires counting an unbounded amount of information and therefore is **not** regular (cannot be recognised by any FSM).
choices: Balanced parentheses nested to arbitrary depth
Strings of any length that start with the prefix "ab"
Binary strings containing an equal number of 0s and 1s
$a^n b^n$ for an arbitrary number $n$
questions.explanation: Balanced parentheses need to track nesting depth, equal-0s-and-1s needs a running difference, and $a^n b^n$ needs to remember exactly how many $a$s were seen — all unbounded as the input grows, so none is regular. Starting with a fixed two-symbol prefix only needs a few states, so it **is** regular.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT part of the standard Turing machine model?
choices: An infinite tape divided into cells
A head that reads, writes and moves one cell left or right at a time
A finite table of transition rules
Random-access memory addressable by an index
questions.explanation: A Turing machine only ever accesses its tape one cell at a time via the head's current position — it has no random-access memory that can jump straight to an arbitrary index. Tape, head, states and transition rules are the whole model.

questions.text: What is the core reason no general algorithm can solve the halting problem for every program and input?
choices: Modern computers don't have enough memory to run every possible program
Assuming such an algorithm exists lets you build a program that contradicts whatever answer it gives, however it answers
The halting problem has simply never been attempted by computer scientists
It would require exponential time, which is considered too slow
questions.explanation: The proof is a self-reference argument: assuming a correct `halts` checker exists, you can build a program `trouble` that does the opposite of what `halts` predicts about itself, so whichever answer `halts` gives is wrong — a contradiction. This shows no such algorithm can exist, regardless of speed or memory.

questions.text: Select every statement below that is TRUE.
choices: Whether a given FSM accepts a given input string is decidable
Whether an arbitrary program halts on an arbitrary input is decidable
Whether a given number is prime is decidable
Whether two arbitrary programs always produce identical output on every input is decidable
questions.explanation: Tracing an FSM's finite transitions always finishes (decidable), and primality can always be checked and terminates (decidable). Both the halting problem and general program-equivalence are proven undecidable — no algorithm can answer them correctly for every case.

questions.text: An algorithm whose running time is proportional to $2^n$ for an input of size $n$ is best described as:
choices: Tractable
Intractable
questions.explanation: Exponential running time ($2^n$) grows so fast that even modest inputs become infeasible to compute in practice — this is the definition of intractable, in contrast to polynomial-time (e.g. $n^2$) tractable algorithms.

questions.text: Give the term for a problem for which no algorithm exists that is guaranteed to always terminate with a correct answer (write one word).
questions.explanation: Such a problem is called **undecidable** — the halting problem is the classic example, proved by a self-reference argument.
